1. affinity scheduling reduce cache misses because the thread/processor is executed on the right processor which preserve the cache data, executing on it again can use the cache data, which reduce the cache miss.

3. affinity scheduling doesn't influence page fault rate, because the page is either in shared physical RAM or backing storage.

1. Affinity scheduling executes thread on the same cpu, where it was executed before. That means that it affects TLB misses and local cache (specific cpu dedicated cache) misses, because these are located in the  cpu.
2. However it doesn't affect page faults, because all cpu's use the same RAM. Memory either is located on the physical frame or not, it doesn't depend on the CPU. The same goes for global cache (cache that is available for all cpus) misses, since they are not dependent on specific CPU.
